summary: A biographical and philosophical overview of Martin Heidegger, focusing on his rejection of Cartesian dualism in favor of 'Dasein' (bodily immersion in the world). The text outlines his distinction between 'ready-to-hand' and 'present-at-hand' modes of existence, while also addressing his controversial involvement with the Nazi party.

principles.statement: Cognition and language are firmly based on our innate capacities and bodily immersion in the world.
principles.rationale: Human beings are not detached minds distinct from their bodies, but feeling entities totally engaged with their environment.
principles.source_quote: ...he saw cognition and the use of language as firmly based on our innate capacities and on a bodily immersion in the world.

principles.statement: Everyday skills take precedence over the detached intellect.
principles.rationale: We primarily relate to the world through practical, unthinking engagement with tools and our environment, rather than through abstract, conscious observation.
principles.source_quote: ...he sought to slaughter the most sacred cow of philosophy, the preeminence of the intellect over everyday skills.

mental_models.name: Dasein (Being-there)
mental_models.description: A model of human existence that rejects the idea of a detached mind. Instead, it defines a human as a feeling entity that is immersed in and totally engaged with the everyday world.
mental_models.example: When asking 'What is Being?', one must look at what it is to be human in an everyday, engaged sense, rather than analyzing humanity as an isolated, abstract concept.

mental_models.name: Ready-to-hand (zuhanden) vs. Present-at-hand (vorhanden)
mental_models.description: Two distinct ways we relate to entities in the world. 'Ready-to-hand' is when we use an object skillfully for a purpose without conscious thought. 'Present-at-hand' is when we view an object as an isolated physical thing requiring conscious thought, usually because its usefulness has been interrupted.
mental_models.example: A skilled craftsman wielding a hammer uses it without conscious thought (ready-to-hand). If the hammer breaks, the pieces become a collection of new objects requiring conscious thought to figure out their usefulness (present-at-hand).

signature_quotes.text: To think is to confine yourself to a single thought that one day stands still like a star in the world’s sky.
signature_quotes.context: Written in 1947 and inscribed on his gravestone.

signature_quotes.text: ...expected from National Socialism a spiritual renewal of life in its entirety, a reconciliation of social antagonisms and a deliverance of Dasein from the dangers of communism.
signature_quotes.context: Written in a 1948 letter to former student Herbert Marcuse, explaining his initial motivations for joining the Nazi party.

anti_patterns.description: Cartesian dualism (viewing the mind as distinct from the body it inhabits).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It ignores that human cognition is based on bodily immersion in the world and falsely elevates detached intellect over everyday practical skills.

anti_patterns.description: Treating philosophy as the handmaiden of science.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It distracts philosophy from its ancient, original concerns, specifically the fundamental question of 'What is Being?' and the nature of everyday human experience.
